claim: Ignoring Hand B entirely, Hand A text exhibits internal linguistic coherence: (1) consonant skeletons take multiple suffixes (inflection-like), (2) plant folios have folio-specific bigram distributions beyond the _.oii category tag, and (3) LDA topic structure correlates with section labels. If all three hold, Hand A supports linguistic analysis as a natural-language-like system. If not, Hand A is a code, cipher, or non-linguistic encoding.
evidence_for: Hand A's _.oii closed-class tag behavior (H-BV-CONSONANT-ID-01) is consistent with a functional morpheme in a natural language
Natural languages produce inflectional diversity (same stem with multiple endings), so this is a clean falsifiable test
If Hand A is linguistically coherent, Brain-V's decoding effort should focus exclusively on it and treat Hand B as a separate system
evidence_against: Hand A _.oii's 3-stem closed class could also reflect a cipher-level category marker rather than a linguistic morpheme
Four consecutive refutations of cross-hand semantics suggest Brain-V may find similar nulls internally
Medieval Latin has strong inflection but not strong suffix cross-product; the test may over-predict
test:
LOCKED THREE-PART TEST within Hand A only:
  (1) INFLECTION: for each consonant skeleton appearing >=5 times in Hand A, count distinct final-suffix chars attached from {y,n,r,m,g}. Report mean and median suffixes per skeleton; % of skeletons with >=2 distinct suffixes.
  (2) PLANT-FOLIO SPECIFICITY: for each Hand-A plant folio vs each non-plant herbal folio, compute folio-level word-bigram distribution. Mean pairwise Jaccard top-50 bigrams within plant folios vs across plant/non-plant.
  (3) LDA TOPIC STRUCTURE: run Latent Dirichlet Allocation with K=8 on Hand-A folio-level bag-of-words. For each topic, compute section-label purity (fraction of top-20 high-weight folios in a single section). Report mean top-20 section purity across topics.
test_threshold: CONFIRMED if: (1) mean suffixes per skeleton >= 2.0 AND >=50% of skeletons take >=2 suffixes, (2) plant-vs-plant Jaccard >= 1.3x plant-vs-nonplant, (3) mean per-topic section purity >= 0.60. If all three pass: natural-language-like. REFUTED if any fails: code/cipher/non-linguistic.

tests_run.details: ALL 3 pass. (1) Inflection: 162 frequent skeletons, mean 2.46 distinct suffixes, 69.8%% take >=2 (PASS >=2.0 & >=50%%). (2) Plant bigram specificity: within 0.0014 vs across 0.0010, ratio 1.44x (PASS >=1.3x). (3) LDA K=8: mean top-20 section purity 0.831 (PASS >=0.60), 6 of 8 topics >=80%% pure, topic 2 100%% herbal. Hand A supports linguistic analysis under locked rules.

tests_remaining: Execute locked test in separate commit
reasoning: After four refutations of cross-hand semantics, Brain-V pivots to testing internal coherence of one hand. If Hand A alone is linguistically coherent, that's the surface on which decoding effort should concentrate. If not, Brain-V accepts Hand A is a code/cipher and the linguistic-decoding approach is the wrong tool.
